title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You have read a newspaper article arguing that learning foreign languages is no longer necessary because translation technology is so advanced. Write a letter to the newspaper editor disagreeing with this view, explaining the benefits of learning foreign languages, and arguing why language learning should remain a priority in education.

Write your letter to the editor.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I read your recent article on translation technology with great interest, but I must respectfully challenge the conclusion that this renders foreign language learning unnecessary. This argument, while superficially plausible, overlooks the many profound benefits that language learning provides beyond mere communication.

Learning a foreign language is, fundamentally, a gateway to understanding another culture. It enables us to appreciate literature, humour, history, and social nuance in a way that no automated translation tool can replicate. A machine can translate words; it cannot convey the texture and depth of human expression.

From a cognitive perspective, research has consistently demonstrated that bilingual and multilingual individuals exhibit stronger memory, greater mental flexibility, and delayed onset of dementia. These are not trivial benefits.

Professionally, employers across a wide range of sectors actively seek candidates with language skills, viewing them as evidence of cultural awareness, discipline, and intellectual capability.

I urge schools and policymakers to resist the temptation of technological shortcuts and maintain language learning at the heart of our educational system.

Yours sincerely,
A Language Teacher

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt:
You have been asked to write a review for an English-language education website. Write a review of a language-learning app or language course you have used. Describe what it offers, evaluate how effective it is for improving language skills, and say whether you would recommend it to other learners.

Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
LinguaFlow App — The Smart Way to Learn a Language?

Having used LinguaFlow consistently for four months in my attempt to improve my Spanish, I can now offer a balanced and honest review of its strengths and limitations.

The app's greatest asset is its adaptive learning algorithm, which personalises lessons based on your performance, targeting your weakest areas with impressive accuracy. The interface is clean and intuitive, making it easy to fit in short practice sessions during a commute or lunch break. Vocabulary and grammar are introduced gradually and reinforced through spaced repetition, which genuinely aids long-term retention.

However, speaking practice remains the app's most significant weakness. While it offers basic pronunciation exercises, these feel superficial compared to what you would gain from even occasional conversation with a native speaker. The app is best understood as a complement to other learning methods, not a standalone solution.

For complete beginners and intermediate learners looking to consolidate their knowledge, LinguaFlow is excellent value and highly motivating. For those seeking conversational fluency, I would recommend supplementing it with a language exchange programme or regular tutoring sessions.

Overall rating: 4 out of 5.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English friend Morgan has just started learning English and is finding it difficult. Write an email to Morgan giving practical tips on how to improve their English outside the classroom, explaining what has worked well for you personally, and encouraging them to keep going.

Write your email to Morgan.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Morgan,

I was so pleased to hear that you have started learning English — it is such a rewarding journey, even if it feels overwhelming at times! I completely understand the frustration, so I wanted to share a few things that have genuinely helped me.

First, try to immerse yourself in English as much as possible outside lessons. Watch films and series in English with subtitles — not your own language, but English subtitles. This trains your ear and your eye simultaneously. I started with shows I already knew well, which made following the dialogue much easier.

Secondly, keep a vocabulary notebook and write down new words in sentences, not just in isolation. Context makes words far easier to remember.

I also strongly recommend finding a language exchange partner. Websites like Tandem or HelloTalk connect you with native English speakers who want to learn your language — it is free, fun, and incredibly effective for building confidence.

Most importantly, do not be afraid of making mistakes. They are genuinely how you learn.

You are doing brilliantly — keep going!

Warm wishes,
Ella
